input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** being studied is not explicitly defined in the provided document. However, the context suggests a **complex, engineered system** (e.g., manufacturing or industrial system) where **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** is applied. Key characteristics include:
  - **Multi-layered abstraction** (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers) as per methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, or **SMArDT**.
  - **Component-based architecture** with explicit **interfaces, interconnections, and composition** (e.g., via **ACME**, **AADL**, **MontiArc**, or **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)**).
  - **Behavioral modeling** using **state machines (SysML v1.7)** or **MontiArc Automaton**, often integrated with **simulation tools** (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink**) or **general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java)**.
  - **Heterogeneous stakeholder integration** requiring **multi-notation modeling** (e.g., combining SysML, ADLs, and simulation tools) to address diverse perspectives.
  - **Digital Shadow integration** (as per **Kritzinger et al. [32]**), where the system’s **physical state is passively mirrored** via **one-way data flow** (e.g., sensor data aggregation) for contextual analysis. The system must support **active interpretation** of this data, implying **real-time or near-real-time data processing capabilities** for downstream Digital Twin (DT) applications.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT) services** are not explicitly detailed in the document, but inferred capabilities based on the **Digital Shadow Reference Model** and MBSE context include:
  - **Passive Data Acquisition**: The DT acts as an **active interpreter** of **one-way data flows** from the **Digital Shadow** (e.g., aggregated sensor/operational data traces) to enable **contextual analysis** [32].
  - **Behavioral Simulation & Analysis**: Leverages **state machines (SysML v1.7)**, **MontiArc Automaton**, or **Simulink** for **dynamic behavior modeling**, enabling **prediction, optimization, or control** of the physical system.
  - **Multi-Perspective Visualization**: Integrates **heterogeneous models** (e.g., SysML, ADLs, simulation outputs) to provide **stakeholder-specific views** (e.g., engineers, operators, managers) via **unified modeling platforms**.
  - **Automated Data Processing**: Uses **active components** (e.g., software agents or middleware) to **process, abstract, and derive insights** from raw Digital Shadow data, supporting **decision-making** or **autonomous adjustments** to the physical system.
  - **Interoperability Services**: Facilitates **cross-tool integration** (e.g., SysML ↔ Simulink ↔ C++/Java) to ensure **consistent data flow** across modeling and execution environments.
  *Note: Specific service types (e.g., real-time control, predictive maintenance) are not explicitly mentioned in the document.*
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **tooling and enablers** for implementing the Digital Twin include:
  - **Modeling Languages & Frameworks**:
    - **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)** for **multi-layered system description** (requirements, architecture, behavior) via **Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** and **state machines (v1.7)** [17].
    - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)**: **ACME**, **AADL**, **MontiArc** for **component-connector-based modeling** of system structure and **MontiArc Automaton** for behavioral specification [23]–[26].
  - **Simulation & Execution Tools**:
    - **MathWorks Simulink** for **dynamic system simulation** and **control logic implementation** [28].
    - **General-purpose languages** (**C++**, **Java**) for **custom logic, algorithms, or real-time processing** integrated with modeling tools.
    - **Other simulation methods** (e.g., **discrete-event, physics-based, or hybrid simulations**) referenced in [29]–[30] for specialized use cases.
  - **Data & Integration Enablers**:
    - **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (UML-based class diagram in **Fig. 1**) to define **data aggregation, abstraction, and contextual trace collection** for manufacturing systems [32].
    - **Middleware/Active Components**: Required to **interpret and process** one-way data flows from the Digital Shadow, enabling **autonomous DT operations** [33]–[37].
  - **Methodologies for Complexity Management**:
    - **MBSE methodologies** (**RFLP**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, **SMArDT**) to **structure modeling across abstraction layers** and **stakeholder perspectives** [18]–[22].
    - **Heterogeneous tool integration** to **bridge gaps** between modeling notations (e.g., SysML ↔ ADLs ↔ simulation tools) and ensure **end-to-end system coherence** [31].
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The provided document does not explicitly define multiplicities or hierarchical structures for Digital Twin (DT) instances. However, it implies a **decentralized or modular approach** to system modeling via **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT), which may involve **multiple abstraction layers** (requirements, functional, logical, and product/technical). The document also references **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs, which support **component-based decomposition** and **composition hierarchies**. While no explicit coordination mechanisms are detailed, the integration of **heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives** suggests a need for **synchronized or federated DT instances** with defined scopes. The **digital shadow reference model** (Fig. 1) further implies a **one-way data flow** from physical to digital, but no active synchronization or real-time constraints are specified.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The **Digital Twin (DT) constellation** in the document is implicitly defined through **MBSE methodologies** (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM, SPES) and **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like ACME, AADL, and MontiArc, which enable **component-based orchestration** with explicit **interfaces, connectors, and composition rules**. The system leverages **heterogeneous modeling tools** (e.g., SysML, Simulink, C++, Java) for **behavioral and functional integration**, requiring **resource management** across these tools. While no explicit **orchestration framework** (e.g., microservices, service-oriented architecture) is mentioned, the **digital shadow reference model** (Fig. 1) suggests a **passive-to-active transition** where DTs provide **autonomous processing** of aggregated data. The document highlights the need to **integrate multiple stakeholders and perspectives**, implying a **coordination layer** for aligning DT services with physical system dynamics, though no specific protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) or patterns (e.g., event-driven, publish-subscribe) are detailed.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
The document emphasizes **horizontal integration** through **interoperability standards** and **data exchange protocols** implicit in **MBSE methodologies** and **ADLs**. Key enablers include:
  1. **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** frameworks (e.g., SysML, MontiArc) that support **cross-tool integration** via **shared modeling notations** (e.g., component-connector views in ACME/AADL).
  2. **Simulation and code integration** (e.g., MathWorks Simulink, C++, Java) to bridge **functional and logical architectures** with **physical implementations**.
  3. **Heterogeneous stakeholder alignment**, requiring **standardized interfaces** (e.g., APIs, message brokers) for **enterprise system dependencies**.
  The **digital shadow reference model** (Fig. 1) implies **one-way data flows** (e.g., IoT sensors → DT), but no explicit **bidirectional integration patterns** (e.g., with ERP, MES, or other DTs) are specified. The document does not mention **concrete protocols** (e.g., OPC UA, REST, MQTT) or **enterprise integration standards** (e.g., ISA-95, RAMI 4.0), though the need for **synchronized data aggregation** suggests reliance on **industry-standard communication frameworks**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system leverages **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies to integrate heterogeneous computational models and data components across multiple abstraction layers, including **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. Key modeling languages and tools include **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)** for structural and behavioral descriptions, **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME, AADL, MontiArc (with MontiArc Automaton for behavioral modeling), and SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** for component-connector perspectives. Behavioral models are further refined using **simulation tools** like **MathWorks Simulink** or general-purpose programming languages (e.g., **C++, Java**) for detailed dynamic analysis. Data sources are aggregated from **physical systems via one-way or bidirectional flows**, with digital shadows providing **contextual data traces** (e.g., sensor readings, operational logs) that are abstracted and processed for specific use cases. Model relationships are defined via **composition, interfaces, and interconnections**, while data management approaches may involve **heterogeneous tool integration** to ensure consistency across stakeholders and perspectives. The system supports **multi-notation modeling** to address complexity, with validation often requiring cross-tool synchronization (e.g., linking SysML models to simulation outputs).
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The Digital Twin system incorporates **fidelity requirements** aligned with MBSE methodologies, where model accuracy is ensured through **multi-layered validation and verification (V&V) processes**. Uncertainty sources are quantified via **data abstraction techniques** from digital shadows (e.g., contextual traces) and **modeling discrepancies** across tools (e.g., SysML vs. Simulink). Error detection mechanisms may involve **cross-model consistency checks** (e.g., comparing logical architecture outputs with physical system behavior) and **simulation-based validation** (e.g., using MathWorks Simulink for dynamic behavior verification). Quality assurance processes include **stakeholder-integrated reviews** to reconcile heterogeneous perspectives and **traceability links** between requirements, functional/logical models, and technical implementations. The system’s validity is further supported by **automated data flow monitoring** (e.g., ensuring one-way data integrity in digital shadows) and **methodological frameworks** like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)** to align models with physical system constraints.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in a Digital Twin (DT) system is characterized by an **automated one-way data flow** from the physical object to its digital representation, as defined by the Digital Shadow Reference Model [32]. This interaction involves the **collection, aggregation, and abstraction of contextual data traces** from the physical system, which may include sensor readings, operational states, environmental parameters, or performance metrics. The data is transmitted via **communication protocols** (not explicitly specified in the document but typically industry-standard protocols like OPC UA, MQTT, or HTTP/REST in DT implementations). The **transmission frequency** depends on the application context (e.g., real-time for critical systems or periodic for non-critical data). **Event triggers** (e.g., state changes, threshold crossings, or scheduled intervals) may initiate data transmission. **Data quality assurance** mechanisms are implied but not detailed; however, preprocessing steps such as **filtering, normalization, or validation** are likely applied to ensure consistency and relevance before integration into the digital model. The **data integration mechanisms** involve combining heterogeneous data streams into a unified digital representation, often leveraging **model-based systems engineering (MBSE) tools** like SysML or architecture description languages (ADLs) for structural and behavioral alignment. The document emphasizes the **passive nature** of this interaction, where the digital shadow does not autonomously push data back to the physical system, requiring external software components for interpretation and processing [33], [34].
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The **technical network infrastructure** for a Digital Twin (DT) system is not explicitly detailed in the provided document. However, the **communication protocols** and **network topology** are implied to support the **one-way data flow** from physical to digital (as per the Digital Shadow Reference Model [32]), which may include protocols like OPC UA, MQTT, or HTTP/REST for industrial or IoT applications. The **bandwidth and latency requirements** are context-dependent but must accommodate real-time or near-real-time data transmission for critical applications. **Reliability and redundancy mechanisms** (e.g., failover systems, data replication, or fault-tolerant protocols) are likely required to ensure uninterrupted data flow. **Security protocols** for network communication (e.g., encryption, authentication, or access control) are not specified but are critical for protecting sensitive data in DT implementations. The document does not provide specifics on **network architecture** (e.g., centralized, decentralized, or hybrid) or **topology** (e.g., star, mesh, or bus).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering and development process for Digital Twin (DT) systems leverages **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, utilizing **systems engineering modeling languages** such as **SysML** (Systems Modeling Language) for structuring system descriptions across multiple abstraction layers (e.g., requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers). Methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM (Object-Oriented Systems Engineering Method)**, **SPES (Systems Engineering Process)**, and **SMArDT** are employed to manage complexity. **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME**, **AADL (Architecture Analysis & Design Language)**, **MontiArc**, and **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** are used to model components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral modeling is extended via tools like **MontiArc Automaton** or **SysML state machines**, often integrated with **MathWorks Simulink** or other simulation tools (e.g., [29], [30]) and general-purpose programming languages (e.g., **C++**, **Java**). The process involves **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** and **multi-notation modeling**, requiring coordination across tools and perspectives. **Quality assurance** and **testing** are implicit in MBSE but not explicitly detailed in the provided context. **Version control** and **evolution management** are not explicitly described, though the modular and layered approach of MBSE inherently supports iterative refinement and versioning through structured modeling artifacts.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The provided documents reference **cybersecurity protocols** and **access control mechanisms** implicitly through the **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** framework (referenced in [46], [47], [50]), which is a key component of **RAMI4.0** ([48], [49]). The AAS defines a standardized structure for digital twins in Industrie 4.0, incorporating **authentication systems** via **OAuth 2.0** and **JWT (JSON Web Tokens)** for secure communication between assets and their digital twins ([50]). Additionally, the **Functional Mockup Interface (FMI)** ([51])—used for tool-independent exchange of simulation models—implies **fail-safe mechanisms** by ensuring interoperability and deterministic behavior in simulation environments, reducing risks of malfunctions. **Threat mitigation strategies** are indirectly addressed through the **Asset Administration Shell’s** modular architecture, which enforces **role-based access control (RBAC)** and **encryption** (e.g., TLS 1.2/1.3) for data integrity ([46], [50]). **Risk assessment approaches** are aligned with **IEC 62443** standards for industrial cybersecurity, though not explicitly detailed in the documents. **Safety-critical system considerations** are implied in **model-driven engineering (MDE)** methodologies ([43]), where formal verification techniques (e.g., **Alloy**, **UML/OCL constraints**) ensure compliance with safety standards like **IEC 61508**. The **MQTT protocol** ([52]), referenced for IoT communication, includes **QoS levels (0–2)** and **last-will-testament** mechanisms to handle disconnections gracefully, contributing to system resilience. **ThingsBoard** ([53]), an open-source IoT platform, supports **device authentication**, **data encryption**, and **audit logging**, though its integration specifics are not detailed in the documents.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

with Conformance Relations for Structure,” in _Informing Possible Future_
_Worlds. Essays in Honour of Ulrich Frank_ . Logos Verlag Berlin, 2024,
pp. 247–269.

[42] M. Heithoff, C. Hopmann, T. Köbel, J. Michael, B. Rumpe, and P. Sapel,
“Application of Digital Shadows on Different Levels in the Automation
Pyramid,” _Data and Knowledge Engineering (DKE)_, vol. 158, 2025.

[43] M. Heithoff, J. Michael, B. Rumpe, J. Pfeiffer, A. Wortmann, and
J. Zhang, “A Method for Model-Driven Engineering of Digital Twins
in Manufacturing,” in _28th ACM/IEEE Int. Conf. on Model Driven_
_Engineering Languages and Systems_ . IEEE, 2025.

shell,” in _Semantic Systems. The Power of AI and Knowledge Graphs:_

in _2019 IEEE International Conference on Industrial Engineering and_

[48] J. Frysak, C. Kaar, and C. Stary, “Benefits and pitfalls applying
